subject: economics
title: Scarcity and Opportunity Cost
stem_template: A government has a limited budget of ${{budget}} billion. It chooses to spend it on education rather than healthcare. The opportunity cost of this decision is:
answer_template: The value of the healthcare that must be forgone — opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative sacrificed when a choice is made.
distractor_templates: The monetary cost of the education spending (${{budget}} billion).
The future economic growth generated by better-educated workers.
Zero — governments face no opportunity cost when spending on public goods.

options_template.text: The value of the healthcare foregone — next best alternative sacrificed.
The monetary cost of the education spending (${{budget}} billion).
The future economic growth from better-educated workers.
Zero — governments face no opportunity cost.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for 'value of healthcare foregone' or 'next best alternative sacrificed'. Must reference healthcare specifically.
hints: Opportunity cost = next best alternative foregone.
It is not the monetary cost of what was chosen.

subject: economics
title: Factors of Production
stem_template: A factory building, a piece of machinery, and a computer are all examples of which factor of production?
answer_template: Capital — manufactured goods used in the production of other goods and services.
distractor_templates: Land — natural resources used in production.
Labour — human effort (physical and mental) used in production.
Enterprise — the risk-taking ability to organise the other factors of production.

options_template.text: Capital — manufactured goods used to produce other goods and services.
Land — natural resources used in production.
Labour — human effort used in production.
Enterprise — risk-taking to organise other factors.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for Capital with correct definition (manufactured/physical goods used in production).
hints: Capital in economics means physical capital (machines, buildings), not money.
Land includes all natural resources; Labour is human effort.

subject: economics
title: Production Possibility Frontier Analysis
stem_template: An economy can produce either {{good_A}} or {{good_B}} using all its resources. Currently it produces {{q_A}} units of {{good_A}} and {{q_B}} units of {{good_B}}. Explain what a point inside the PPC, on the PPC, and outside the PPC represents.
answer_template: Inside PPC: productive inefficiency — resources are unemployed or misallocated; output could be increased without sacrificing any good. On PPC: productive efficiency — all resources fully and efficiently employed; to produce more of one good, output of the other must be reduced (opportunity cost). Outside PPC: currently unattainable — requires better technology, more resources, or improved productivity.
mark_scheme_template: Award 2 marks each: inside (inefficiency + reason), on (efficiency + opportunity cost), outside (unattainable + reason). Max 6.
hints: PPC = Production Possibility Curve/Frontier.
Only movement along the PPC involves opportunity cost.

subject: economics
title: Positive vs Normative Statements
stem_template: Which of the following is a normative economic statement?
answer_template: 'The government should increase the minimum wage to reduce poverty.' — This expresses a value judgment (what ought to be done), not a factual claim.
distractor_templates: 'An increase in the minimum wage leads to higher unemployment among low-skilled workers.' — This is a positive statement (testable claim).
'The inflation rate in 2023 was 4%.' — This is a factual, positive statement.
'Higher interest rates reduce consumer borrowing.' — This is a testable, positive statement.

options_template.text: 'The government should increase the minimum wage to reduce poverty.' — normative (value judgment).
'An increase in minimum wage leads to higher unemployment.' — positive (testable).
'Inflation in 2023 was 4%.' — positive (factual).
'Higher interest rates reduce borrowing.' — positive (testable).

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for the normative statement ('should') + correct explanation (value judgment, cannot be tested empirically).
hints: Normative = 'should/ought'; based on value judgments.
Positive = factual, testable claim regardless of whether it is true.

subject: economics
title: Ceteris Paribus
stem_template: Economists use the assumption of ceteris paribus when building economic models. What does this mean?
answer_template: 'All other things being equal/held constant' — it allows economists to study the effect of one variable by assuming all other relevant variables remain unchanged.
distractor_templates: Consumers always make rational decisions that maximise their utility.
Markets always reach equilibrium in the long run.
Economic models must include all real-world variables to be valid.

options_template.text: 'All other things equal/held constant' — isolates the effect of one variable.
Consumers always maximise utility rationally.
Markets always reach long-run equilibrium.
Models must include all real-world variables.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for 'all other things equal/constant' + purpose (isolating one variable's effect).
hints: Latin: ceteris paribus = 'other things being equal'.
It is a simplifying assumption, not a description of reality.

subject: economics
title: Evaluating Economic Models
stem_template: A simplified economic model assumes that consumers are perfectly rational and that markets are always in equilibrium. Discuss two advantages and two limitations of using such simplified models.
answer_template: Advantages: 1. Simplification allows economists to identify causal relationships between variables (e.g. price → quantity demanded) without being overwhelmed by complexity. 2. Models generate testable predictions that can be compared with real-world data. Limitations: 1. Ceteris paribus assumption is unrealistic — in reality many variables change simultaneously. 2. Behavioural economics shows consumers are not always rational (anchoring, loss aversion), reducing model accuracy.
mark_scheme_template: Award 2 marks per well-developed point (1 for statement, 1 for elaboration). Max 8.
hints: Advantages: simplicity, testability, identifies relationships.
Limitations: unrealistic assumptions, ceteris paribus, behavioural biases.

subject: economics
title: Demand Determinants — Shift vs Movement
stem_template: The price of {{good}} falls from ${{p1}} to ${{p2}}. Assuming ceteris paribus, this will cause:
answer_template: A movement along the demand curve (extension of demand) — a fall in price of {{good}} increases quantity demanded, shown as a movement down the existing demand curve.
distractor_templates: A rightward shift of the demand curve — more is demanded at every price.
A leftward shift of the demand curve — less is demanded at every price.
No change in demand — price changes do not affect demand for necessities.

options_template.text: A movement along the demand curve (extension) — lower price → higher quantity demanded.
A rightward shift of the demand curve.
A leftward shift of the demand curve.
No change — necessities are price-insensitive.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for 'movement along demand curve' (not shift) + direction (extension/down-right).
hints: Price changes cause movements along the demand curve, not shifts.
Non-price determinants (income, taste, substitutes) cause shifts.

subject: economics
title: Demand Shifters — Income Change
stem_template: Consumer incomes rise by {{income_rise}}%. For a normal good, this will cause the demand curve to:
answer_template: Shift rightward — for a normal good, higher income increases demand at every price level, shifting the demand curve to the right.
distractor_templates: Shift leftward — higher income reduces demand as consumers switch to premium substitutes.
Move along the curve — income changes cause movements, not shifts.
Remain unchanged — demand depends only on price, not income.

options_template.text: Shift rightward — higher income increases demand for normal goods at all prices.
Shift leftward — consumers switch to premium substitutes.
Move along the curve — income causes movements, not shifts.
Remain unchanged — demand depends only on price.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for rightward shift + correct reasoning (normal good, more income → more demand).
hints: Normal goods: income and demand move in the same direction.
Inferior goods: demand falls as income rises (e.g. instant noodles).

subject: economics
title: Demand Diagram and Analysis
stem_template: Draw and explain a demand diagram for {{good}}, showing the effect of a {{change}} on demand. Explain the direction of any shift and identify two other factors that could cause a similar shift.

values: fall in the price of a complement
rise in the price of a substitute

answer_template: Diagram: downward-sloping demand curve. {{change}} → rightward shift (D₁ to D₂) as demand increases at all prices. Two other shifters: (1) change in consumer tastes/fashion; (2) change in price of related goods (substitutes/complements). Each must be explained with reference to the direction of shift.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark labelled diagram (P, Q, D); 1 mark correct shift direction; 1 mark reason for shift; 1 mark per additional factor (max 2). Max 5.
hints: Label axes (Price, Quantity) and the demand curve.
Shifts in demand: non-price factors only.

subject: economics
title: Supply Shifters
stem_template: The cost of raw materials used to produce {{good}} increases by {{pct}}%. Assuming ceteris paribus, the supply curve for {{good}} will:
answer_template: Shift leftward — higher production costs reduce profitability, so producers supply less at every price level.
distractor_templates: Shift rightward — higher material costs motivate producers to supply more to cover costs.
Move along the supply curve — an extension of supply occurs at higher prices.
Remain unchanged — supply depends only on the final product price.

options_template.text: Shift leftward — higher costs reduce supply at every price.
Shift rightward — firms supply more to cover higher costs.
Move along the curve — an extension of supply at higher prices.
Remain unchanged — only product price affects supply.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for leftward shift + correct reason (higher costs → reduced profitability → less supply at each price).
hints: Higher costs = supply decreases (leftward shift).
A rise in the product's own price causes a movement along supply (extension).

subject: economics
title: Supply Curve Shape
stem_template: Why is the supply curve typically upward-sloping (positive relationship between price and quantity supplied)?
answer_template: Higher prices make production more profitable, incentivising existing firms to produce more and attracting new firms into the market.
distractor_templates: Higher prices increase consumer demand, which forces producers to supply more.
The supply curve slopes upward because costs always increase with output.
Producers supply more at higher prices to earn enough to cover their fixed costs.

options_template.text: Higher prices increase profitability, incentivising more production and new entry.
Higher prices increase consumer demand, forcing producers to supply more.
Costs always increase with output, so higher prices needed to cover them.
Producers supply more to cover fixed costs at higher prices.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for profitability incentive + possibility of new firm entry.
hints: The profit motive is central to supply behaviour.
Distinguish between incentive to supply more vs cost justification.

subject: economics
title: Technology Improvement and Supply
stem_template: A new automated production technology reduces the cost of producing {{good}} by {{cost_fall}}%. Using a supply and demand diagram, explain the effect on the market equilibrium price and quantity.
answer_template: Lower production costs → supply curve shifts rightward (S₁ → S₂). With demand unchanged, the new equilibrium has a lower price and higher quantity. Diagram should show: original equilibrium (P₁, Q₁); rightward shift of supply; new equilibrium (P₂, Q₂) where P₂ < P₁ and Q₂ > Q₁.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark labelled diagram; 1 mark rightward supply shift; 1 mark lower price; 1 mark higher quantity; 1 mark reason (lower costs increase profitability/supply). Max 5.
hints: Technology improvements lower costs, shifting supply right.
When supply increases (rightward shift), price falls and quantity rises.

subject: economics
title: Market Equilibrium — Shortage and Surplus
stem_template: In a competitive market for {{good}}, the current price is ${{price}} but the equilibrium price is ${{eq_price}}. Since the current price is {{comparison}} the equilibrium, there is a market {{condition}}.
answer_template: Price above equilibrium → surplus (QS > QD); downward pressure on price toward equilibrium. Price below equilibrium → shortage (QD > QS); upward pressure on price toward equilibrium.
distractor_templates: Price above equilibrium → shortage; firms must raise output to meet excess demand.
Price below equilibrium → surplus; consumers bid price down.
Any price deviation has no self-correcting mechanism in competitive markets.

options_template.text: Above equilibrium → surplus (QS > QD); price falls toward equilibrium.
Above equilibrium → shortage; firms raise output.
Below equilibrium → surplus; consumers bid price down.
Markets have no self-correcting mechanism.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correctly identifying surplus/shortage AND the direction of price adjustment.
hints: Surplus = supply > demand; price must fall to clear excess.
Shortage = demand > supply; price rises until market clears.

subject: economics
title: Simultaneous Shifts in Supply and Demand
stem_template: In the market for {{good}}, both {{demand_shift}} (shifting demand {{d_dir}}) and {{supply_shift}} (shifting supply {{s_dir}}) occur simultaneously. Analyse the effect on equilibrium price and quantity.

values: a major producer exits the market

answer_template: When demand shifts right and supply shifts left (or right), the effect on price and quantity depends on the relative size of shifts. Right demand + left supply: price definitely rises; quantity change is indeterminate (could rise, fall, or stay the same). Right demand + right supply: quantity definitely rises; price change is indeterminate. Must draw both diagrams showing each scenario.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per correct shift diagram (max 2); 1 mark definite outcome stated correctly; 1 mark indeterminate outcome identified with reasoning. Max 5.
hints: Draw two separate diagrams: one for each shift.
When both curves shift, at least one of P or Q will be indeterminate.

subject: economics
title: Bounded Rationality
stem_template: Behavioural economists argue that consumers often exhibit 'bounded rationality'. What does this mean?
answer_template: Consumers have limited information, cognitive ability, and time; they make 'satisficing' decisions (good enough) rather than fully optimising, often relying on heuristics and biases.
distractor_templates: Consumers always make rational decisions but are constrained by their budget.
Consumers are irrational in all their decisions and cannot be predicted.
Governments must always intervene to correct consumer irrationality.

options_template.text: Limited information, cognition, and time lead consumers to satisfice using heuristics, not fully optimise.
Consumers are rational but budget-constrained.
Consumers are always irrational and unpredictable.
Government must always intervene to correct irrationality.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for limited information/cognition/time + satisficing or heuristics.
hints: Herbert Simon coined 'bounded rationality'.
Satisficing = choosing an option that is 'good enough', not optimal.

subject: economics
title: Nudge Theory Application
stem_template: A government changes the default option for organ donation from 'opt-in' to 'opt-out'. This policy relies on which behavioural economics concept?
answer_template: Default bias (status quo bias) — people tend to stick with the default option; changing the default increases organ donation rates without restricting freedom of choice (nudge theory).
distractor_templates: Loss aversion — people fear losing organs more than they value saving lives.
Anchoring bias — the initial information about organ donation sets a reference point.
Rational choice theory — individuals maximise utility by choosing the socially optimal outcome.

options_template.text: Default bias/status quo bias — people stick with the default; nudge theory preserves choice.
Loss aversion — fear of losing organs outweighs benefits.
Anchoring bias — initial information sets a reference point.
Rational choice — individuals choose the socially optimal outcome.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for default bias/status quo bias + link to nudge theory (preserves choice).
hints: Nudges change the choice architecture without restricting options.
Default bias: inertia leads people to stick with the default.

subject: economics
title: Critique of Rational Consumer Model
stem_template: Evaluate the view that the standard economic assumption of rational, utility-maximising consumers should be replaced by a behavioural economics approach in designing government policy for {{policy_area}}.
answer_template: For replacement: evidence shows systematic biases (present bias, loss aversion, default bias) in {{policy_area}}; nudges can achieve policy goals at low cost without coercion; traditional incentives (taxes/subsidies) may fail if irrationality is the root cause. Against: behavioural interventions may be paternalistic, manipulative, and undermine autonomy; traditional economic tools (taxes, regulations) have clearer, more predictable effects; biases vary across individuals. Conclusion: hybrid approach likely best.
mark_scheme_template: Award up to 3 marks for arguments in favour; 3 marks against; 2 marks for balanced conclusion. Max 6.
hints: Name specific biases relevant to {{policy_area}}.
A good evaluation includes both sides and a justified conclusion.

subject: economics
title: Price Elasticity of Demand — Definition
stem_template: The price of {{good}} rises by {{pct_p}}% and quantity demanded falls by {{pct_q}}%. The price elasticity of demand (PED) is:
answer_template: \\( PED = \\dfrac{\\% \\Delta Q_d}{\\% \\Delta P} = \\dfrac{-{{pct_q}}}{{{pct_p}}} = {{ped}} \\). Since |PED| {{comparison}} 1, demand is {{elasticity}}.
distractor_templates: \\( PED = {{pct_p}} / {{pct_q}} = {{pos_ped}} \\) (positive), demand is elastic.
\\( PED = {{pct_q}} / {{pct_p}} \\) (absolute value), elasticity cannot be determined from this data.
PED = 1 in all markets (unit elastic by definition).

options_template.text: \\( PED = -{{pct_q}}/{{pct_p}} = {{ped}} \\); |PED| {{comparison}} 1 → {{elasticity}}.
PED = {{pct_p}}/{{pct_q}} (positive value); elastic.
PED = {{pct_q}}/{{pct_p}}; elasticity indeterminate.
PED = 1 (unit elastic by definition).

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correct formula; 1 mark correct calculation; 1 mark correct classification (elastic/inelastic).
hints: PED = (%ΔQd) / (%ΔP). Always negative for normal goods.
|PED| > 1 = elastic; |PED| < 1 = inelastic; |PED| = 1 = unit elastic.

subject: economics
title: PED Calculation and Revenue Impact
stem_template: The price of {{good}} rises from ${{p1}} to ${{p2}}. Quantity demanded falls from {{q1}} to {{q2}} units. (a) Calculate the PED. (b) Determine whether a price rise increases or decreases total revenue.

tr_change.formula: Math.abs(ped) > 1 ? 'falls (price rise harms revenue when elastic)' : 'rises (price rise boosts revenue when inelastic)'

answer_template: \\( \\% \\Delta P = \\dfrac{{{p2}}-{{p1}}}{{{p1}}} \\times 100 = {{pct_p}}\\% \\). \\( \\% \\Delta Q = \\dfrac{{{q2}}-{{q1}}}{{{q1}}} \\times 100 = {{pct_q}}\\% \\). \\( PED = {{pct_q}} / {{pct_p}} = {{ped}} \\). TR before: ${{tr1}}. TR after: ${{tr2}}. Since |PED| {{comparison}} 1 ({{elasticity}}), TR {{tr_change}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark %ΔP; 1 mark %ΔQ; 1 mark PED formula and calculation; 1 mark TR comparison; 1 mark TR conclusion with elasticity reasoning. Max 5.
hints: PED = (%ΔQd)/(%ΔP). Elastic demand: price↑ → TR↓. Inelastic: price↑ → TR↑.
Calculate percentage changes before dividing.

subject: economics
title: Income and Cross-Price Elasticity
stem_template: Consumer incomes rise by {{inc_pct}}% and demand for {{good_A}} rises by {{q_pct}}%. Simultaneously, the price of {{good_B}} rises by {{pb_pct}}% and demand for {{good_A}} rises by {{xed_q}}%. (a) Calculate YED for {{good_A}}. (b) Calculate XED between {{good_A}} and {{good_B}}. (c) State whether {{good_A}} is normal/inferior and whether {{good_A}} and {{good_B}} are substitutes/complements.
answer_template: \\( YED = \\dfrac{{{q_pct}}}{{{inc_pct}}} = {{yed}} \\). Positive YED > 0 → normal good. \\( XED = \\dfrac{{{xed_q}}}{{{pb_pct}}} = {{xed}} \\). Positive XED → substitutes; Negative XED → complements.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark YED calculation; 1 mark normal/inferior; 1 mark XED calculation; 1 mark substitutes/complements. Max 4.
hints: YED > 0 = normal good; YED < 0 = inferior good.
XED > 0 = substitutes; XED < 0 = complements.

subject: economics
title: Price Elasticity of Supply — Determinants
stem_template: Which factor is most likely to make the supply of {{good}} price elastic (PES > 1)?
answer_template: Spare production capacity — firms can quickly increase output without large cost increases; also: ease of factor substitution, short production period, and availability of stocks.
distractor_templates: A long production period — this makes supply inelastic as it takes time to increase output.
High barriers to entry — new firms cannot enter quickly to increase supply.
Large fixed capital investment required — this makes supply rigid and inelastic.

options_template.text: Spare production capacity — output can be raised quickly at low extra cost.
Long production period — takes time to increase output (inelastic).
High barriers to entry — new firms cannot quickly enter.
Large fixed capital investment — supply is rigid and inelastic.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for spare capacity OR any valid determinant of elastic supply with correct reasoning.
hints: Elastic supply: easy, cheap, quick to increase output.
Inelastic supply: requires time, capital investment, or faces natural constraints.

subject: economics
title: PES Calculation
stem_template: The price of {{good}} rises from ${{p1}} to ${{p2}} and quantity supplied increases from {{q1}} to {{q2}} units. Calculate the Price Elasticity of Supply (PES) and classify whether supply is elastic or inelastic.
answer_template: \\( \\% \\Delta P = \\dfrac{{{p2}}-{{p1}}}{{{p1}}} \\times 100 = 25\\% \\). \\( \\% \\Delta Q_s = \\dfrac{{{q2}}-{{q1}}}{{{q1}}} \\times 100 \\approx 10\\% \\). \\( PES = 10/25 = 0.4 \\). Since PES < 1, supply is price inelastic.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark %ΔP; 1 mark %ΔQs; 1 mark PES calculation; 1 mark classification (elastic/inelastic). Max 4.
hints: PES = (%ΔQs) / (%ΔP). Always positive (direct relationship).
PES > 1 = elastic; PES < 1 = inelastic; PES = 1 = unit elastic.

subject: economics
title: Tax Incidence and PES
stem_template: A government imposes a specific tax of ${{tax}} per unit on {{good}}. Explain how the price elasticity of supply and demand jointly determine who bears the greater burden of this tax (producers or consumers).
answer_template: Tax incidence depends on relative elasticities. If demand is inelastic (consumers insensitive to price) and supply is elastic: consumers bear most of the tax (price rises by close to ${{tax}}). If demand is elastic and supply is inelastic: producers bear more (price rises little; producers absorb the cost). Diagram should show: supply shifts left by ${{tax}}; new equilibrium P and Q; consumer surplus loss (consumer burden) vs producer surplus loss (producer burden).
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark diagram (leftward supply shift by tax amount); 1 mark consumer burden (inelastic demand); 1 mark producer burden (inelastic supply); 1 mark quantification of share; 1 mark general principle. Max 5.
hints: The more inelastic the side of the market, the more tax burden they bear.
Draw supply shift first, then identify how much price rises vs. how much the producer receives after tax.

subject: economics
title: Price Ceiling Effects
stem_template: A government imposes a maximum price (price ceiling) of ${{ceiling}} on {{good}}, which has an equilibrium price of ${{eq_price}}. What is the likely market outcome?
answer_template: A shortage (excess demand) — at the below-equilibrium ceiling price of ${{ceiling}}, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. This may lead to black markets, queuing, and reduced quality.
distractor_templates: A surplus — the ceiling price is below equilibrium, so producers oversupply.
No effect — price ceilings only affect markets when set above the equilibrium price.
The market reaches a new equilibrium at the ceiling price without any imbalance.

options_template.text: Shortage (excess demand) — below-equilibrium ceiling → QD > QS → black markets, queuing.
Surplus — ceiling below equilibrium causes oversupply.
No effect — ceilings only matter above equilibrium.
New equilibrium at ceiling price with no imbalance.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for shortage + explanation (ceiling below equilibrium → QD > QS). Bonus for black market / rationing consequence.
hints: A price ceiling below equilibrium is binding; above equilibrium is non-binding.
Binding ceiling → shortage → potential black markets.

subject: economics
title: Specific Tax — Consumer and Producer Burden
stem_template: A specific tax of ${{tax}} per unit is imposed on {{good}}. After the tax, the price paid by consumers rises from ${{p_eq}} to ${{p_consumer}} and the price received by producers falls to ${{p_producer}}. Calculate: (a) the tax per unit borne by consumers, (b) the tax per unit borne by producers, and (c) the government's total tax revenue if {{q_tax}} units are sold.
answer_template: Consumer burden = ${{p_consumer}} − ${{p_eq}} = ${{c_burden}} per unit. Producer burden = ${{p_eq}} − ${{p_producer}} = ${{p_burden}} per unit. Total tax revenue = ${{tax}} × {{q_tax}} = ${{revenue}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark consumer burden; 1 mark producer burden; 1 mark total revenue calculation. Max 3.
hints: Consumer burden = rise in price paid. Producer burden = fall in price received.
Tax revenue = tax per unit × quantity sold.

subject: economics
title: Subsidy Analysis
stem_template: A government grants a subsidy of ${{subsidy}} per unit to producers of {{good}}. Using a supply and demand diagram, analyse the effects on the market price paid by consumers, the price received by producers, quantity traded, and government expenditure.
answer_template: Subsidy shifts supply rightward by ${{subsidy}} (producers willing to supply more at each price). New equilibrium: consumer price falls (Pc < original equilibrium); producer price rises (Pp = Pc + {{subsidy}} > original equilibrium). Quantity traded increases. Government expenditure = ${{subsidy}} × new quantity. Diagram shows: rightward supply shift, new equilibrium, wedge between Pc and Pp of ${{subsidy}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark diagram (rightward supply shift); 1 mark lower consumer price; 1 mark higher producer price; 1 mark increased quantity; 1 mark government expenditure formula. Max 5.
hints: Unlike a tax, a subsidy shifts supply rightward (reduces producer costs).
The wedge between consumer and producer price equals the subsidy amount.

subject: economics
title: Negative Externality — Definition
stem_template: A factory producing {{good}} releases pollutants into a nearby river, harming local fishermen. This is an example of:
answer_template: A negative externality of production — the factory imposes costs on third parties (fishermen) not involved in the market transaction; social marginal cost exceeds private marginal cost → overproduction.
distractor_templates: A positive externality — the factory benefits the community by providing employment.
A public good problem — the river is non-excludable and non-rival.
Asymmetric information — the fishermen do not know about the pollution.

options_template.text: Negative externality of production — third-party costs; SMC > PMC; overproduction.
Positive externality — employment benefit to community.
Public good problem — river is non-excludable and non-rival.
Asymmetric information — fishermen unaware of pollution.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for negative externality + third party/external cost + SMC > PMC.
hints: Externality = effect on third parties not in the market transaction.
Negative externality of production: SMC > PMC → market overproduces.

subject: economics
title: Public Goods Characteristics
stem_template: Which pair of characteristics correctly defines a public good?
answer_template: Non-excludable (impossible to prevent non-payers from consuming) and non-rival (one person's consumption does not reduce availability for others), leading to the free-rider problem.
distractor_templates: Excludable and rival — like private goods, available only to paying customers.
Non-excludable and rival — like common resources (fish in open ocean).
Excludable and non-rival — like club goods (streaming services, toll roads).

options_template.text: Non-excludable and non-rival → free-rider problem.
Excludable and rival — private goods.
Non-excludable and rival — common resources.
Excludable and non-rival — club goods.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for both non-excludable AND non-rival + free-rider problem or market failure consequence.
hints: Examples: national defence, lighthouse, street lighting.
Free-rider problem → private markets underprovide public goods.

subject: economics
title: Correcting Negative Externalities
stem_template: Evaluate two government policies to correct the negative externality caused by {{polluter}} in the market for {{product}}. Include a diagram showing the deadweight welfare loss before intervention.
answer_template: Diagram: PMC below SMC; market output Q_market > socially optimal Q_opt; deadweight welfare loss shown. Policy 1 — Pigouvian tax (= external cost per unit): internalises the externality by raising PMC to SMC; output falls to Q_opt. Advantage: efficient. Disadvantage: difficult to measure external cost precisely. Policy 2 — regulation (cap on emissions): directly limits output to Q_opt. Advantage: certain output reduction. Disadvantage: no incentive for further reduction; compliance cost. Evaluation: taxes give more flexibility and revenue; regulation is more certain.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark diagram with SMC/PMC/DWL; 1 mark each policy explanation; 1 mark advantage/disadvantage each; 1 mark comparative evaluation. Max 7.
hints: Pigouvian tax = optimal tax set equal to external cost per unit.
Show DWL as triangle between Q_opt and Q_market under the SMC/PMC gap.

subject: economics
title: GDP Measurement Approaches
stem_template: Which of the following is correctly counted in a country's Gross Domestic Product (GDP)?
answer_template: The value of new goods and services produced within the country's borders during the year (including by foreign-owned firms operating domestically).
distractor_templates: The value of all goods traded (including second-hand goods) within the country.
Income earned by citizens abroad plus income earned domestically.
The total stock of wealth held by residents of the country.

options_template.text: Value of new goods/services produced within the country's borders (including by foreign firms).
Value of all goods traded, including second-hand goods.
Citizens' domestic income plus income earned abroad.
Total stock of resident wealth.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for: new production + within borders + current year/period. Must exclude second-hand, transfer payments, and financial transactions.
hints: GDP = production within borders; GNI = income of citizens (including abroad).
Second-hand goods and financial transactions are not included in GDP.

subject: economics
title: Real vs Nominal GDP
stem_template: Country A's nominal GDP grew from ${{gdp1}} billion in Year 1 to ${{gdp2}} billion in Year 2. The price level (GDP deflator) rose from 100 to {{deflator}}. (a) Calculate real GDP in Year 2 (base year = Year 1). (b) Determine whether the economy actually grew in real terms.
answer_template: \\( \\text{Real GDP}_2 = \\dfrac{\\text{Nominal GDP}_2}{\\text{Deflator}/100} = \\dfrac{{{gdp2}}}{{{deflator}}/100} = \\${{real_gdp2}} \\text{ bn} \\). Since real GDP in Year 2 ({{real_gdp2}}) {{comparison}} GDP in Year 1 ({{gdp1}}), the economy {{grew_or_shrunk}} in real terms.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark deflator formula; 1 mark correct real GDP calculation; 1 mark correct comparison and conclusion. Max 3.
hints: Real GDP = Nominal GDP / (Price Index/100).
Real GDP removes the distortion of price level changes.

subject: economics
title: Limitations of GDP as a Welfare Measure
stem_template: Evaluate GDP per capita as a measure of living standards and economic wellbeing in {{country}}. Suggest two alternative or supplementary indicators.
answer_template: GDP per capita gives an average that hides income distribution (Gini coefficient needed); excludes non-market activities (household work, volunteerism); ignores environmental degradation; doesn't capture health, education, or political freedoms. Alternative indicators: Human Development Index (HDI) — combines income, education, and life expectancy; Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI) — adjusts for inequality, crime, and environmental costs; Happiness Index (e.g. World Happiness Report).
mark_scheme_template: Award: 2 marks per limitation (1 for issue, 1 for explanation) max 4; 1 mark per alternative indicator with description, max 2. Max 6.
hints: GDP per capita hides inequality — two countries can have the same GDP/capita but very different distributions.
HDI is the most common IB alternative indicator.

subject: economics
title: Types of Unemployment
stem_template: Workers in the {{industry}} industry lose their jobs because new technology has replaced their roles. This is an example of:
answer_template: Structural unemployment — caused by a mismatch between the skills of workers and the needs of employers; often the result of technological change or industrial restructuring.
distractor_templates: Cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment — caused by a fall in aggregate demand during a recession.
Frictional unemployment — workers temporarily between jobs while searching for new employment.
Seasonal unemployment — workers losing jobs due to predictable seasonal demand fluctuations.

options_template.text: Structural unemployment — skills mismatch due to technology/industrial change.
Cyclical unemployment — fall in aggregate demand during recession.
Frictional unemployment — temporarily between jobs during job search.
Seasonal unemployment — predictable seasonal fluctuations.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for structural unemployment + skills mismatch / technological change reason.
hints: Structural = caused by fundamental changes in the economy's structure.
Cyclical = caused by recession; Frictional = voluntary job-searching.

subject: economics
title: Inflation — CPI Measurement
stem_template: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rises from {{cpi1}} to {{cpi2}} over a year. What is the inflation rate?
answer_template: \\( \\text{Inflation rate} = \\dfrac{{{cpi2}} - {{cpi1}}}{{{cpi1}}} \\times 100 = {{inflation}}\\% \\)
distractor_templates: \\( {{cpi2}} - {{cpi1}} = {{diff}} \\) (not a percentage).
Inflation cannot be calculated from CPI values alone.

options_template.text: \\( \\dfrac{{{cpi2}}-{{cpi1}}}{{{cpi1}}} \\times 100 = {{inflation}}\\% \\)
\\( {{cpi2}} - {{cpi1}} \\) absolute difference only.
Cannot be calculated from CPI alone.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correct formula; 1 mark correct numerical answer {{inflation}}%.
hints: Inflation = (CPI_new − CPI_old) / CPI_old × 100.
CPI measures the price level of a representative basket of goods.

subject: economics
title: AD Components
stem_template: Aggregate Demand (AD) is expressed as AD = C + I + G + (X − M). Which of the following would cause a rightward shift of the AD curve?
answer_template: An increase in consumer confidence leading to higher consumption (C) — this raises total spending, shifting AD to the right at all price levels.
distractor_templates: A rise in the price level — this causes a movement along the AD curve, not a shift.
A rise in interest rates leading to reduced investment (I) — this shifts AD leftward.
An increase in imports (M) — this reduces net exports (X−M), shifting AD leftward.

options_template.text: Rise in consumer confidence → higher C → rightward AD shift.
Rise in price level → movement along AD, not a shift.
Rise in interest rates → lower I → leftward AD shift.
Rise in imports → lower (X−M) → leftward AD shift.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correct AD component + correct direction (rightward shift).
hints: Price level changes cause movements along AD; non-price factors shift AD.
AD = C + I + G + (X − M). All four components can shift the curve.

subject: economics
title: Keynesian Multiplier Calculation
stem_template: In an economy, the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is {{mpc}}. The government increases spending by ${{delta_g}} billion. (a) Calculate the multiplier. (b) Calculate the total change in national income (GDP) resulting from this increase.
answer_template: \\( \\text{Multiplier} = \\dfrac{1}{1 - MPC} = \\dfrac{1}{1 - {{mpc}}} = {{multiplier}} \\). \\( \\Delta Y = \\text{Multiplier} \\times \\Delta G = {{multiplier}} \\times {{delta_g}} = \\${{delta_y}} \\text{ bn} \\).
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark multiplier formula; 1 mark correct multiplier value {{multiplier}}; 1 mark total income change ${{delta_y}} bn. Max 3.
hints: Multiplier = 1/(1 − MPC) = 1/MPS.
Higher MPC → higher multiplier → larger income change per $ of government spending.

subject: economics
title: Keynesian vs Monetarist AD-AS Analysis
stem_template: Using AD-AS diagrams, compare the Keynesian and monetarist views on the effects of a {{shock}} on the economy in both the short run and long run.
answer_template: Keynesian: SRAS is horizontal at low output (wages and prices are sticky downward); a fall in AD leads to a large fall in output with little price change; the economy may stay in recession indefinitely without intervention. Monetarist: LRAS is vertical at the natural rate of output (NAIRU); any fall in AD leads to short-run fall in output but wages and prices adjust, restoring LRAS equilibrium; government intervention causes inflation without lasting output gain.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark Keynesian diagram; 1 mark Monetarist LRAS diagram; 1 mark Keynesian short-run explanation; 1 mark Monetarist long-run adjustment; 1 mark comparison of policy implications. Max 6.
hints: Keynesians emphasise price/wage stickiness; monetarists assume flexible wages in the long run.
Draw both Keynesian (horizontal SRAS) and monetarist (vertical LRAS) models.

subject: economics
title: Macroeconomic Objectives — Trade-offs
stem_template: A government pursues policies to reduce unemployment, which leads to an increase in inflation. This illustrates which macroeconomic concept?
answer_template: The Phillips Curve trade-off — in the short run, lower unemployment tends to be associated with higher inflation, as increased employment raises wages and consumer spending, pushing up prices.
distractor_templates: The Laffer Curve — tax cuts can increase tax revenue by stimulating economic growth.
The multiplier effect — government spending increases aggregate demand by more than the initial injection.
Crowding out — government borrowing increases interest rates, reducing private investment.

options_template.text: Phillips Curve — short-run trade-off between unemployment and inflation.
Laffer Curve — tax cuts can increase revenue.
Multiplier effect — spending increases AD by more than initial injection.
Crowding out — government borrowing raises interest rates.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for Phillips Curve + correct description of unemployment-inflation trade-off.
hints: Phillips Curve: inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation (short run).
In the long run, the Phillips Curve may be vertical (natural rate of unemployment).

subject: economics
title: Conflicts Between Macroeconomic Objectives
stem_template: Analyse the potential conflicts between {{obj1}} and {{obj2}} as macroeconomic objectives. Discuss whether these objectives must necessarily conflict and suggest policies that might achieve both simultaneously.
answer_template: Potential conflict: e.g., economic growth may increase inequality (if benefits concentrated in high earners) or worsen environment (increased production/consumption). However, conflict is not inevitable: supply-side policies can raise potential output without inflation; green investment can generate jobs while improving sustainability; redistributive fiscal policy can address inequality without harming growth. Policy examples: education/training (supply-side), carbon taxes (environmental + revenue), progressive taxation (equity + growth-neutral if well-designed).
mark_scheme_template: Award: 2 marks conflict explanation with example; 2 marks why not necessarily in conflict; 2 marks policy recommendations. Max 6.
hints: Not all macroeconomic objectives conflict — supply-side improvements can achieve several simultaneously.
Evaluate both short-run and long-run perspectives.

subject: economics
title: Fiscal Policy — Expansionary vs Contractionary
stem_template: An economy is experiencing a recession with high unemployment. Which fiscal policy is most appropriate and why?
answer_template: Expansionary fiscal policy — increase government spending and/or cut taxes to boost aggregate demand (AD shifts right), increasing output and employment via the multiplier effect.
distractor_templates: Contractionary fiscal policy — cut government spending to reduce the budget deficit during recession.
Neutral fiscal policy — maintain constant spending and taxes regardless of the economic cycle.
Monetary tightening — raise interest rates to control inflation caused by the recession.

options_template.text: Expansionary fiscal policy — higher G and/or lower taxes → AD rightward → output and employment rise.
Contractionary fiscal policy — cut spending to reduce deficit.
Neutral fiscal policy — constant spending/taxes.
Monetary tightening — raise interest rates.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for expansionary + reason (stimulate AD/output/employment) + mechanism (multiplier or AD shift).
hints: Recession → output below potential → expansionary policy to close negative output gap.
Multiplier amplifies the initial spending injection.

subject: economics
title: Budget Balance Calculation
stem_template: Government spending (G) = ${{G}} bn. Tax revenue (T) = ${{T}} bn. Transfer payments = ${{TP}} bn. (a) Calculate the budget balance. (b) State whether this is a surplus or deficit and its size.

surplus_deficit.formula: balance >= 0 ? 'Budget surplus of $' + Math.abs(balance) + ' bn.' : 'Budget deficit of $' + Math.abs(balance) + ' bn.'

answer_template: Total public expenditure = G + TP = ${{total_exp}} bn. Budget balance = T − total_exp = ${{balance}} bn. {{surplus_deficit}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark total expenditure (G + TP); 1 mark budget balance formula; 1 mark correct surplus/deficit classification. Max 3.
hints: Budget balance = Tax revenue − (Government spending + Transfer payments).
Deficit = expenditure > revenue; surplus = revenue > expenditure.

subject: economics
title: Monetary Policy — Interest Rate Transmission
stem_template: A central bank raises its policy interest rate from {{r1}}% to {{r2}}%. Explain the transmission mechanism to aggregate demand.
answer_template: Higher interest rates → (1) borrowing more expensive → less consumer credit and mortgage spending (C falls); (2) less business investment as cost of capital rises (I falls); (3) exchange rate appreciates as capital flows in → exports more expensive, imports cheaper (X falls, M rises); → AD falls → downward pressure on inflation.
distractor_templates: Higher rates directly increase government spending on debt interest, expanding AD.
Higher rates raise the exchange rate, making exports cheaper and boosting AD.
Higher rates have no effect on AD if consumers save the additional interest income.

options_template.text: Higher rates → less borrowing (↓C, ↓I) + exchange rate appreciation (↓X) → AD falls → inflation pressure reduced.
Higher rates increase government debt interest → expand AD.
Higher rates appreciate exchange rate → cheaper exports → AD rises.
Higher rates have no AD effect if consumers save interest income.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per transmission channel (max 3): consumption, investment, exchange rate.
hints: Higher interest rates: discourage borrowing, encourage saving, appreciate currency.
The exchange rate channel is important — appreciate → exports fall, imports rise.

subject: economics
title: Limitations of Monetary Policy
stem_template: Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy in addressing {{economic_problem}} in a {{country_type}} economy.
answer_template: Monetary policy can be effective when: interest rates have room to move; banking system is well-developed; transmission mechanism works. Limitations: 'pushing on a string' — low rates may not stimulate if confidence is low (liquidity trap); time lags (12–18 months); not effective for structural unemployment (wrong tool); may not work for supply-side inflation; in small open economies, exchange rate effects can be significant but unpredictable; developing economies may lack functional bond markets.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 2 marks effectiveness (conditions and mechanism); 2 marks limitations (liquidity trap, lags, structural issues); 2 marks evaluation of context. Max 6.
hints: The liquidity trap occurs when interest rates are near zero — cuts have no further effect.
Consider the specific context ({{country_type}}) for relevant limitations.

subject: economics
title: Supply-Side Policies — Examples
stem_template: Which of the following is a supply-side policy designed to increase an economy's productive potential?
answer_template: Investment in education and training — this increases human capital, raising labour productivity and shifting LRAS (potential output) to the right.
distractor_templates: Increasing government spending during a recession — this is demand-side fiscal policy.
Cutting interest rates to stimulate consumer borrowing — this is monetary policy.
Imposing import tariffs to protect domestic industries — this is trade policy affecting AD, not LRAS.

options_template.text: Investment in education and training — increases human capital → higher productivity → LRAS shifts right.
Increasing government spending during recession — demand-side fiscal policy.
Cutting interest rates to stimulate borrowing — monetary policy.
Imposing import tariffs — trade policy, shifts AD not LRAS.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for supply-side policy + link to LRAS/productive potential. Accept: deregulation, privatisation, R&D subsidies, labour market reform.
hints: Supply-side policies aim to shift the LRAS (long-run aggregate supply) rightward.
They target the supply side of the economy (productivity, costs, incentives).

subject: economics
title: Supply-Side Policy Evaluation
stem_template: Evaluate the effectiveness of {{policy}} as a supply-side policy in improving economic performance. Include potential advantages and disadvantages.
answer_template: {{policy}}: Advantages — may increase efficiency, incentivise investment, reduce costs, attract FDI, raise potential output. Disadvantages — time lags (10–20 years for education); may increase inequality (lower taxes benefit high earners); deregulation can reduce worker protection; privatisation may not improve efficiency in natural monopolies; infrastructure investment has high upfront cost. Overall: supply-side policies best suit long-run objectives but are insufficient for short-run stabilisation — must be combined with demand-side policies.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 2 marks advantages; 2 marks disadvantages; 2 marks evaluation/conclusion. Max 6.
hints: Time lags are a major weakness of supply-side policies.
Consider distributional effects (who gains, who loses).

subject: economics
title: Comparative Advantage
stem_template: Country A can produce either {{units_a_good1}} units of {{good1}} or {{units_a_good2}} units of {{good2}} per day. Country B can produce either {{units_b_good1}} units of {{good1}} or {{units_b_good2}} units of {{good2}} per day. Which country has a comparative advantage in producing {{good1}}?
answer_template: Comparative advantage in {{good1}}: Country A opportunity cost = {{oc_a}} units of {{good2}} per unit of {{good1}}. Country B opportunity cost = {{oc_b}} units of {{good2}} per unit of {{good1}}. Whichever country has the lower opportunity cost has the comparative advantage.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark correct OC for Country A; 1 mark correct OC for Country B; 1 mark correct comparative advantage identification. Max 3.
hints: Comparative advantage = lower opportunity cost (not absolute output).
OC of good1 in terms of good2 = units of good2 / units of good1 that can be produced.
options_template: Country A, because its opportunity cost of {{good1}} is lower ({{oc_a}} < {{oc_b}})
Country B, because its opportunity cost of {{good1}} is lower ({{oc_b}} < {{oc_a}})
Country A, because it can produce more {{good1}} in absolute terms
Neither country has a comparative advantage
distractor_templates: Country B, because its opportunity cost of {{good1}} is lower ({{oc_b}} < {{oc_a}})
Country A, because it can produce more {{good1}} in absolute terms
Neither country has a comparative advantage

subject: economics
title: Gains from Trade and Specialisation
stem_template: Using the concept of comparative advantage, explain why both countries can gain from trade even if one country ({{country_A}}) has an absolute advantage in producing both {{good1}} and {{good2}}.
answer_template: Absolute advantage means {{country_A}} can produce more of both goods with the same resources. But comparative advantage depends on opportunity cost. Even if {{country_A}} is more efficient at both, if it is relatively more efficient at {{good1}}, it should specialise in {{good1}} and trade. The other country specialises in {{good2}} (where its relative disadvantage is smaller). Total world output increases; both countries consume beyond their individual PPC through trade.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark defines comparative advantage (OC); 1 mark distinguishes from absolute advantage; 1 mark explains specialisation pattern; 1 mark explains increased world output/consumption beyond PPC; 1 mark practical example. Max 5.
hints: It is not absolute productivity that matters — it is relative opportunity cost.
Both countries can consume beyond their own PPC after specialising and trading.

subject: economics
title: Tariffs vs Quotas
stem_template: A government imposes a tariff of {{tariff}}% on imported {{good}}. What is the effect on domestic consumers compared to a quota that restricts imports to the same quantity?
answer_template: Both a tariff and a quota raise the domestic price and reduce import quantity. Key difference: a tariff generates government tax revenue; a quota does not (revenue goes to foreign exporters or import licence holders). With a tariff, domestic producers, consumers, government, and world welfare all change in predictable ways; quota effects depend on how licences are allocated.
distractor_templates: A tariff always reduces domestic prices more than a quota.
A quota generates more government revenue than a tariff for the same import restriction.
Tariffs and quotas have identical economic effects in all circumstances.

options_template.text: Both raise price/reduce imports; key difference: tariff generates government revenue, quota does not.
A tariff reduces domestic prices more than a quota.
A quota generates more revenue than a tariff.
Tariffs and quotas have identical economic effects in all circumstances.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for similarity (both raise price, reduce imports) + key difference (revenue to government vs exporters).
hints: Tariff → government revenue. Quota → 'quota rent' to foreign exporters unless auctioned.
Both are protectionist measures but differ in revenue generation.

subject: economics
title: Trade Protection Diagram Analysis
stem_template: Using a diagram, analyse the welfare effects of a tariff of ${{tariff}} per unit imposed on imports of {{good}}. Identify: consumer surplus loss, producer surplus gain, government revenue, and deadweight welfare loss.
answer_template: Diagram: world price (Pw); domestic supply (S) and demand (D). With tariff: domestic price rises to Pw + {{tariff}}. Consumer surplus falls by area (a+b+c+d). Producer surplus rises by area a. Government revenue = area c (tariff × import quantity). Deadweight welfare loss = areas b + d (production and consumption inefficiency). Net welfare effect: negative for the economy as a whole.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark correct diagram (Pw, Pw+tariff, S, D); 1 mark consumer surplus loss; 1 mark producer surplus gain; 1 mark government revenue; 1 mark DWL triangles b+d; 1 mark net welfare conclusion. Max 6.
hints: Label areas a, b, c, d on the diagram clearly.
DWL from tariff = two triangles: production efficiency loss (b) and consumption efficiency loss (d).

subject: economics
title: Economic Integration — Stages
stem_template: Which type of economic integration goes furthest in removing trade barriers and coordinating economic policy?
answer_template: Economic and monetary union — includes a free trade area, customs union, common market, plus a single currency and coordinated monetary policy (e.g. the Eurozone).
distractor_templates: Free trade area — members remove tariffs with each other but maintain independent external tariffs.
Customs union — members have a common external tariff but no free movement of factors.
Common market — free movement of goods, services, and factors, but separate currencies.

options_template.text: Economic and monetary union — all barriers removed plus single currency and coordinated monetary policy.
Free trade area — internal tariff-free; independent external tariffs.
Customs union — common external tariff; no factor mobility.
Common market — free movement of factors but separate currencies.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for economic/monetary union + reference to single currency or coordinated monetary policy.
hints: Stages: FTA → Customs Union → Common Market → Economic Union → Monetary Union.
The EU is the most advanced example of economic integration.

subject: economics
title: Trade Creation vs Trade Diversion
stem_template: A country joins a customs union with {{partner}}. Explain the concepts of trade creation and trade diversion, and evaluate whether joining the union is likely to increase or decrease economic welfare.

values: a customs union with shared external tariffs

answer_template: Trade creation: when joining the customs union causes the country to replace expensive domestic production with cheaper imports from union members → efficiency gain → welfare increases. Trade diversion: when union membership causes the country to switch from efficient non-member imports (now subject to external tariff) to less efficient member imports → welfare loss. Net welfare effect depends on which effect dominates. Favourable conditions for net gain: large bloc, low external tariff, geographically close and economically similar members.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark trade creation definition + welfare gain; 1 mark trade diversion definition + welfare loss; 1 mark net effect analysis; 1 mark conditions for net gain. Max 4.
hints: Trade creation = replacing costly domestic production with cheaper union imports (good).
Trade diversion = switching from efficient non-member to less efficient member (bad).

subject: economics
title: Exchange Rate Systems
stem_template: Under a floating exchange rate system, the value of the {{currency}} is determined by:
answer_template: Market forces of supply and demand for the currency in the foreign exchange market — no government intervention; the rate adjusts automatically to eliminate balance of payments disequilibria.
distractor_templates: A fixed value set by the central bank, maintained through buying and selling reserves.
A government-set band within which the currency is allowed to fluctuate.
The relative price level between trading partners, as measured by purchasing power parity.

options_template.text: Market supply and demand for the currency — no government intervention; automatic adjustment.
A fixed value maintained by the central bank using reserves.
A government-set band (managed float).
Purchasing power parity between trading partners.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for market forces/supply and demand + no/minimal government intervention.
hints: Floating = determined by market; Fixed = government sets rate; Managed = market within government-set limits.
A floating rate is self-correcting for balance of payments imbalances.

subject: economics
title: Exchange Rate Calculation — Appreciation Effect
stem_template: The exchange rate for the {{currency_pair}} changes from {{er1}} to {{er2}}. An imported good costs {{foreign_price}} in the foreign currency. Calculate the price of the import in domestic currency before and after the exchange rate change.
answer_template: Price before = {{foreign_price}} × {{er1}} = {{price_before}} (domestic currency). Price after = {{foreign_price}} × {{er2}} = {{price_after}} (domestic currency). Appreciation of the foreign currency → imports become more expensive.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark price before {{price_before}}; 1 mark price after {{price_after}}; 1 mark correct direction (appreciation → more expensive imports). Max 3.
hints: Multiply foreign price by exchange rate to get domestic currency price.
Appreciation of foreign currency → domestic currency buys less → imports more expensive.

subject: economics
title: Balance of Payments Accounts
stem_template: A country runs a current account deficit of ${{deficit}} billion. Which statement best explains the implications of this deficit?
answer_template: The country imports more goods, services, and income than it exports; it must finance the deficit through capital/financial account surpluses (borrowing, selling assets, FDI inflows), which may create future debt obligations.
distractor_templates: The country is exporting more than it imports and accumulating foreign exchange reserves.
A current account deficit always leads to an immediate depreciation of the exchange rate.
The government budget must also be in deficit if the current account is in deficit.

options_template.text: More imports than exports; financed by capital/financial account surpluses → future debt obligations.
Exports exceed imports; accumulating reserves.
Always leads to immediate exchange rate depreciation.
Government budget must also be in deficit.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for imports > exports + financed by capital inflows + debt/asset implications.
hints: Current account deficit must be matched by capital/financial account surplus (BOP must balance).
A deficit is not necessarily unsustainable — depends on why it arises.

subject: economics
title: Current Account Deficit — Causes and Remedies
stem_template: Country {{country}} has a persistent current account deficit of {{pct}}% of GDP. Analyse the possible causes and evaluate two policies to reduce the deficit.
answer_template: Causes: uncompetitive domestic industries (high production costs); strong consumer demand boosting imports; overvalued exchange rate making exports expensive; trade structure (e.g. reliance on imported energy/manufactured goods). Policies: (1) Exchange rate depreciation — cheaper exports, dearer imports → Marshall-Lerner condition must hold (sum of PED > 1); J-curve effect in short run. (2) Expenditure-reducing policies (fiscal/monetary tightening) — reduce AD → less import demand, but may cause recession. Trade-off: reducing deficit vs maintaining growth.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 2 marks causes (min 2); 2 marks each policy (mechanism + evaluation). Max 6.
hints: Marshall-Lerner condition: depreciation improves current account if |PED_X| + |PED_M| > 1.
J-curve: short-run worsening before long-run improvement after depreciation.

subject: economics
title: Human Development Index
stem_template: The Human Development Index (HDI) measures development using which three dimensions?
answer_template: Life expectancy at birth (health), mean and expected years of schooling (education), and Gross National Income per capita (standard of living).
distractor_templates: GDP per capita, trade balance, and inflation rate.
Literacy rate, infant mortality, and government expenditure on health.
Carbon emissions per capita, Gini coefficient, and GDP growth rate.

options_template.text: Life expectancy (health), schooling years (education), and GNI per capita (living standards).
GDP per capita, trade balance, and inflation rate.
Literacy rate, infant mortality, and health expenditure.
Carbon emissions, Gini coefficient, and GDP growth.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for all three correct dimensions: health (life expectancy), education (schooling), income (GNI per capita).
hints: HDI: H = health (life expectancy); E = education (schooling); I = income (GNI/capita).
HDI ranges from 0 (low) to 1 (very high).

subject: economics
title: Sustainable Development and Trade-offs
stem_template: Evaluate the view that economic growth is incompatible with sustainable development in {{region}}.
answer_template: Arguments for incompatibility: economic growth in {{region}} historically linked to deforestation, carbon emissions, resource depletion, and biodiversity loss; growth benefits often unequally distributed; 'race to the bottom' in environmental standards to attract FDI. Arguments against: 'Environmental Kuznets Curve' — at higher income levels, societies invest in environmental protection; green growth strategies (renewable energy, circular economy) can decouple GDP growth from emissions; sustainable development goals (SDGs) provide a framework; technology transfer. Conclusion: growth and sustainability can be compatible with appropriate institutions, policies, and investment — but not automatically.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 2 marks for arguments that growth harms sustainability; 2 marks for counterarguments; 2 marks for balanced conclusion with reference to policy/context. Max 6.
hints: Environmental Kuznets Curve: pollution rises then falls as income increases.
Green growth and SDGs are important frameworks to reference.

subject: economics
title: Price Signals and Resource Allocation
stem_template: In a free market, how do rising prices for {{good}} signal producers to increase supply?
answer_template: Rising prices increase profit margins, incentivising existing producers to expand output and attracting new entrants; this self-regulating mechanism is the price signal function of markets.
distractor_templates: Falling prices encourage more production because costs decrease.
Government directive tells producers to raise output.
Producers reduce supply to maintain higher prices permanently.

options_template.text: Rising prices → higher profit margins → incentivise output expansion and new entry.
Falling prices encourage production as costs fall.
Government directives increase supply.
Producers reduce supply to keep prices high.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for profit incentive + new entry/expansion of existing firms.
hints: Price signals: high price = produce more; low price = produce less.
This is the invisible hand mechanism (Adam Smith).

subject: economics
title: Causes of Inflation
stem_template: An economy experiences rising prices caused by a significant increase in consumer spending following large-scale government stimulus. This is an example of:
answer_template: Demand-pull inflation — aggregate demand increases faster than aggregate supply can respond, pulling prices up.
distractor_templates: Cost-push inflation — rising production costs (e.g. oil prices) push prices up.
Monetary inflation — caused exclusively by central bank money printing.
Structural inflation — caused by supply-side rigidities.

options_template.text: Demand-pull inflation — excess AD pulls prices up.
Cost-push inflation — rising costs push prices up.
Monetary inflation — caused only by money printing.
Structural inflation — supply-side rigidities.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for demand-pull + excess aggregate demand pulling prices up.
hints: Demand-pull: too much money chasing too few goods.
Cost-push: supply shock reduces AS, raising prices.

subject: economics
title: Natural Rate of Unemployment
stem_template: The natural rate of unemployment includes which types of unemployment?
answer_template: Frictional and structural unemployment — these exist even in a healthy economy; cyclical unemployment is eliminated at the natural rate (also called the Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment, NAIRU).
distractor_templates: Cyclical and seasonal unemployment only.
All types of unemployment including cyclical.
Only frictional unemployment — structural is always avoidable.

options_template.text: Frictional + structural (NAIRU) — cyclical is zero at natural rate.
Cyclical + seasonal only.
All types including cyclical.
Frictional only — structural is avoidable.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for frictional + structural + NAIRU definition.
hints: Natural rate = frictional + structural (irreducible minimum).
NAIRU = unemployment consistent with stable inflation.

subject: economics
title: Automatic Stabilisers
stem_template: During a recession, unemployment benefits automatically increase and income tax revenues fall without any new government legislation. These are known as:
answer_template: Automatic stabilisers — built-in fiscal mechanisms that automatically reduce the severity of economic fluctuations by increasing spending and reducing taxes during recessions and vice versa during booms.
distractor_templates: Discretionary fiscal policy — government actively decides to increase spending.
Monetary policy automatic stabilisers — interest rates adjust automatically.
Supply-side automatic mechanisms — productivity increases automatically during recessions.

options_template.text: Automatic stabilisers — built-in mechanisms that self-adjust to moderate economic cycles.
Discretionary fiscal policy — active government spending decisions.
Monetary policy automatic stabilisers.
Supply-side automatic mechanisms.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for automatic stabilisers + explanation (no new legislation required; reduce cyclical severity).
hints: Automatic stabilisers: unemployment benefits, progressive taxes.
They are counter-cyclical: expand in recession, contract in boom.

subject: economics
title: Quantitative Easing
stem_template: When a central bank purchases government bonds and other financial assets to inject money into the economy, this unconventional monetary policy is called:
answer_template: Quantitative easing (QE) — used when conventional interest rate cuts are insufficient (near-zero rates); central bank creates money to purchase assets, increasing money supply and encouraging lending/investment.
distractor_templates: Open market operations — routine buying/selling of short-term government bonds to manage interest rates.
Reserve requirement reduction — lowering the fraction of deposits banks must hold.
Helicopter money — direct cash transfers to households.

options_template.text: Quantitative easing — large-scale asset purchases to inject money supply when rates near zero.
Open market operations — routine short-term bond management.
Reserve requirement reduction — lowering deposit holding requirements.
Helicopter money — direct household cash transfers.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for quantitative easing + large-scale asset purchases + money supply injection.
hints: QE is used when interest rates are near zero (liquidity trap).
QE increases commercial bank reserves, encouraging lending.

subject: economics
title: Privatisation as Supply-Side Policy
stem_template: A government sells its state-owned {{industry}} to private investors. The rationale is that private ownership will improve efficiency by:
answer_template: Introducing profit motive and competitive pressure — private firms face incentives to reduce costs and improve quality that state enterprises lack; market mechanisms allocate resources more efficiently than government planning.
distractor_templates: Eliminating all market competition to achieve economies of scale.
Guaranteeing lower prices regardless of market conditions.
Removing the need for government regulation of the industry.

options_template.text: Profit motive and competitive pressure → cost reduction and quality improvement.
Eliminating competition → economies of scale.
Guarantees lower prices regardless of market conditions.
Removes need for all government regulation.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for profit motive + efficiency incentive + market pressure.
hints: Privatisation: from state to private ownership.
Counter-argument: natural monopolies may not benefit from privatisation.

subject: economics
title: Terms of Trade
stem_template: A country's terms of trade is calculated as: \\( ToT = \\dfrac{\\text{Index of export prices}}{\\text{Index of import prices}} \\times 100 \\). If export prices rise by {{pct}}% while import prices remain constant, what happens to the terms of trade?
answer_template: Terms of trade improve (increase) — a higher ToT means each unit of exports buys more imports; the country can purchase more foreign goods for the same quantity of exports.
distractor_templates: Terms of trade worsen — higher export prices make the country less competitive.
Terms of trade are unchanged — import prices are the only determinant.
The country should reduce exports to prevent further ToT improvement.

options_template.text: ToT improves — higher export prices → more imports purchasable per unit of exports.
ToT worsens — higher export prices reduce competitiveness.
ToT unchanged — only import prices matter.
Reduce exports to prevent ToT improvement.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for ToT improves + correct interpretation (more imports per unit of exports).
hints: ToT improves when export prices rise relative to import prices.
Improved ToT = better standard of living if maintained.

subject: economics
title: Infant Industry Argument
stem_template: A developing country imposes a tariff on imported {{good}} to protect its newly established domestic {{good}} industry. This is the:
answer_template: Infant industry argument — temporary protection allows a new domestic industry to grow, achieve economies of scale, and eventually become internationally competitive before tariffs are removed.
distractor_templates: Dumping argument — foreign firms are pricing below cost to drive out domestic firms.
Strategic trade policy — government intervention to promote industries with economies of scale.
Balance of payments argument — protecting imports to reduce current account deficit.

options_template.text: Infant industry argument — temporary protection allows domestic industry to develop and become competitive.
Dumping argument — foreign firms pricing below cost.
Strategic trade policy — promoting economies-of-scale industries.
Balance of payments argument — reducing current account deficit.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for infant industry + temporary protection + future competitiveness goal.
hints: Key word: 'temporary' — infant industry protection should be removed once the industry matures.
Risk: protection may become permanent; inefficient industries sheltered.

subject: economics
title: WTO and Free Trade
stem_template: The World Trade Organization (WTO) promotes free trade through which mechanism?
answer_template: Multilateral trade negotiations — WTO facilitates rounds of negotiations (e.g. Doha Round) to reduce tariffs, quotas, and other trade barriers among member countries; also provides a dispute resolution mechanism.
distractor_templates: Setting a single global tariff rate applied by all member countries.
Imposing sanctions on countries that run trade surpluses.
Directly managing cross-border financial transactions.

options_template.text: Multilateral negotiations to reduce trade barriers + dispute resolution mechanism.
Setting a single global tariff for all members.
Sanctions on countries with trade surpluses.
Managing cross-border financial transactions.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for multilateral negotiations + tariff/barrier reduction + dispute resolution.
hints: WTO: 164 member countries (as of 2024).
WTO replaced GATT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade) in 1995.

subject: economics
title: Fixed Exchange Rate Management
stem_template: Under a fixed exchange rate system, if a country's currency is under downward pressure (demand falls), the central bank must:
answer_template: Buy the domestic currency using foreign exchange reserves (selling foreign currency) to maintain the fixed rate; alternatively, raise interest rates to attract capital inflows and increase demand for the currency.
distractor_templates: Sell domestic currency to reduce its value further.
Impose import controls to reduce demand for foreign currency.
Allow the currency to depreciate freely until a new equilibrium is found.

options_template.text: Buy domestic currency (sell foreign reserves) or raise interest rates to support the fixed rate.
Sell domestic currency to reduce its value further.
Impose import controls to reduce foreign currency demand.
Allow free depreciation to find new equilibrium.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for buying domestic currency using foreign reserves AND/OR raising interest rates.
hints: Downward pressure = supply of domestic currency exceeds demand.
Central bank must intervene to prevent the currency falling below the fixed rate.

subject: economics
title: Capital Account vs Financial Account
stem_template: A foreign company builds a new factory in country X, investing ${{fdi}} million. This transaction is recorded in which component of the Balance of Payments?
answer_template: Financial account — foreign direct investment (FDI) is recorded in the financial account (under direct investment); it is a capital flow representing ownership of productive assets across borders.
distractor_templates: Current account — manufacturing investment generates trade flows.
Capital account — all investment transactions are in the capital account.
Official reserves account — central bank records all foreign investments.

options_template.text: Financial account — FDI (direct investment) is a capital flow recorded here.
Current account — manufacturing generates trade flows.
Capital account — all investment is here.
Official reserves — central bank records foreign investment.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for financial account + FDI/direct investment classification.
hints: Current account: goods, services, income, transfers. Financial account: investment, loans, FDI.
FDI = direct investment; portfolio investment (shares) is also in the financial account.

subject: economics
title: Absolute vs Relative Poverty
stem_template: The World Bank defines extreme poverty as living on less than ${{threshold}} per day (in PPP terms). This is an example of:
answer_template: Absolute poverty — a fixed minimum threshold below which individuals cannot meet their basic needs; does not change relative to society's average income.
distractor_templates: Relative poverty — defined as income below a percentage of the national median income.
Multidimensional poverty — measured using a composite of health, education, and living standards.
Subjective poverty — self-reported inability to afford a desired lifestyle.

options_template.text: Absolute poverty — fixed minimum threshold; does not change with average income.
Relative poverty — below a percentage of national median income.
Multidimensional poverty — composite index of deprivations.
Subjective poverty — self-reported standard.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for absolute poverty + fixed threshold + basic needs definition.
hints: Absolute poverty: fixed dollar threshold. Relative poverty: changes with national income.
World Bank poverty lines: $2.15 (extreme), $3.65 (lower-middle), $6.85 (upper-middle income).

subject: economics
title: Demand Curve Shifters
stem_template: Which of the following will cause the DEMAND CURVE to shift to the RIGHT (increase in demand)? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: An increase in consumer income (normal good)
A fall in the price of the good
An increase in the price of a substitute good
A positive change in consumer tastes towards the good
Expectations of future price increases

mark_scheme_template: Right shift (increase): higher income (normal good); price ↑ of substitute; positive taste change; expected higher future price. WRONG: lower price causes movement ALONG the curve, not a shift.
hints: Price changes = movement along curve, not a shift.
Non-price factors = shift of the demand curve.

subject: economics
title: Market Structure Characteristics
stem_template: Which of the following are characteristics of PERFECT COMPETITION? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: Many buyers and sellers
Firms are price makers
Homogeneous (identical) products
Free entry and exit in the long run
Firms earn supernormal profits in the long run

mark_scheme_template: Perfect competition: many buyers/sellers; homogeneous products; free entry/exit; price TAKERS. FALSE: firms are price takers (not makers); LR economic profit = 0 due to free entry.
hints: Perfect competition: firms accept market price.
SR supernormal profit attracts entry → LR normal profit only.

subject: economics
title: Monetary vs Fiscal Policy
stem_template: Which of the following are examples of EXPANSIONARY monetary policy? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: Reducing interest rates
Increasing government spending
Buying government bonds (open market operations)
Reducing the required reserve ratio for banks
Reducing income tax rates

mark_scheme_template: Expansionary monetary: lower rates; buy bonds (inject money); lower reserve ratio (more lending). NOT monetary: increased government spending and lower taxes are FISCAL policy.
hints: Monetary policy: controlled by central bank, affects money supply/interest rates.
Fiscal policy: government taxation and spending.

subject: economics
title: Price Elasticity of Demand
stem_template: When price rises from \${{P1}} to \${{P2}}, quantity demanded falls from {{Q1}} to {{Q2}} units. Calculate the Price Elasticity of Demand (PED).

steps_template.label: % change in Qd
steps_template.content: \(\% \Delta Q_D = \dfrac{{{Q2}}-{{Q1}}}{{{Q1}}} \times 100\)%. Enter as a decimal (e.g. −0.2 for −20%).

steps_template.label: |PED|
steps_template.content: \(|\text{PED}| = \left|\dfrac{\%\Delta Q_D}{\%\Delta P}\right|\). Enter the absolute value.

mark_scheme_template: \(\%\Delta Q = \dfrac{{{Q2-Q1}}}{{{Q1}}} \approx {{round((Q2-Q1)/Q1*100,1)}}\%\); \(\%\Delta P = \dfrac{{{P2-P1}}}{{{P1}}} \approx {{round((P2-P1)/P1*100,1)}}\%\); \(|\text{PED}| \approx {{round(answer,2)}}\)
hints: PED = (%ΔQd) ÷ (%ΔP)
PED is usually negative for normal goods — take absolute value.

subject: economics
title: AD-AS Analysis Steps
stem_template: Arrange the following steps for analysing the effect of a demand-side shock using the AD-AS model in the CORRECT ORDER.
items: Identify the new equilibrium (price level and real output)
Determine if the shock shifts AD, AS, or both
Describe the economic context and the shock
Show the shift on a labelled AD-AS diagram
mark_scheme_template: Correct order: (1) describe context/shock; (2) identify what shifts; (3) show shift on diagram; (4) identify new equilibrium.
hints: Always start by identifying what is happening in the economy.
Draw the diagram before reading off the new equilibrium.
